title: How Eating Healthier Could Reshape Agriculture
author: Jake Currie
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementEnvironmentHow Eating Healthier Could Reshape AgricultureA Western diet is bad for us and bad for the planetJCBy Jake Currie9:19 AM CDT on July 17, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on Bluesky1CommentsWestern diets—chock-full of fat, sugars, and red meat—are notoriously unhealthy. The production of these foods, which often contributes to habitat loss and greenhouse gas emissions, isn’t exactly great for the planet either. So in 2025 the EAT Foundation teamed up with The Lancet to imagine a better world. Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 10 Running Dinosaurs Probably Wagged Their TailsShareNextStayThe EAT-Lancet Commission tasked experts from a variety of disciplines to create a global diet that would be both healthier for us and healthier for the Earth. The resulting Planetary Health Diet, published in a 2025 report, was a plant-forward diet that strictly limited meats, fats, and sugars. Now, a new study published in Nature looks at how globally adopting the diet would transform agriculture by the year 2050.According to the study, the most dramatic changes would come in the livestock sector, which would see a 70 percent drop in the production value of cattle, sheep, and goats. Vegetable, fruit, and legume production, on the other hand, would increase by 57 percent. On net, these shifts in land use would open up 6 percent of the world’s farmland for other, more environmentally friendly activities.Already have an account?Log inJoin to continue readingYou've run out of free articles.
